literal: blown out (as a flame whose fuel is withdrawn); nir- + √vā ('to blow'), past participle nirvāṇa = 'blown out'; Pali nibbāna (by regular phonological correspondence, -rv- → -bb-).
invariant_definition: *Nirvāṇa* names the going-out of a flame — the image of a fire whose fuel is withdrawn and which therefore ceases to burn (nir- + √vā, 'to blow'; past participle nirvāṇa, 'blown out'; Pali nibbāna). As a soteriological term it is characteristically Buddhist (and Jain): in Buddhism it names the central goal, the going-out of the three fires of *rāga* (greed), *dveṣa* (hatred), and *moha* (delusion), and the unconditioned (*asaṃskṛta*) state freed of craving and rebirth. The early Buddhist usage is apophatic: it is described without positing a permanent self (*anātman*) or a substrate into which one merges. Madhyamaka describes nirvāṇa by eightfold negation ('unabandoned, unattained, uncut-off, non-eternal, unceased, unarisen', MMK 25.3) and holds that there is no distinction between saṃsāra and nirvāṇa (MMK 25.19). The image of a flame going out can be set beside the Vedic and Upaniṣadic imagery of a fire kept and kindled: in the Ṛgveda Agni is the fire seated in the secret place (*guhā*) and found there by seers whose mantras are 'fashioned by the heart', and the Upaniṣads figure the inner self as a flame — 'the Person the measure of a thumb, like a light without smoke' (Kaṭha 2.1.13) — and as the self-luminous 'light of lights' in the heart (Muṇḍaka 2.2.9). The two bodies of imagery share a fire-and-light vocabulary; the Buddhist soteriological term is not attested in the securely pre-Buddhist Hindu texts (see textual history and translator note). The *Bhagavad-Gītā*'s compound *brahma-nirvāṇa* (a separate entry) joins the word to *brahman*: the desire-and-ego fire goes out, and Brahman is what is reached.

school_framing.shared_core: Nirvāṇa as the going-out of the fires of craving — freedom from the burning (*pariḷāha*) of *rāga, dveṣa, moha* — is the core shared by the traditions that use the word. They differ on whether any substrate (self, Brahman, Tathāgatagarbha) underlies or remains after the going-out.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Early Pali and Abhidharma Buddhism describe *nibbāna/nirvāṇa* as the going-out of the fires, the unconditioned (*asaṃskṛta-dhātu*), without positing an *ātman* or *brahman* — an apophatic terminus. Madhyamaka does not assign nirvāṇa the categories 'existent' or 'non-existent' and holds nirvāṇa non-different from saṃsāra (MMK 25.19); some Mahāyāna strands (Tathāgatagarbha) describe a positive substrate. The *Gītā*'s *brahma-nirvāṇa* (separate entry) joins the word to Brahman as the absolute reached. Within Buddhism, Abhidharma treats nirvāṇa as a real unconditioned *dharma* (*asaṃskṛta-dhātu*); Madhyamaka treats that as a further reification and defines nirvāṇa as the stilling of conceptual proliferation (*prapañca*).

per_school.definition: In early Buddhism and the Pali canon, *nibbāna* is the going-out of the three fires — *rāga* (greed), *dveṣa* (hatred), *moha* (delusion) — and the resulting unconditioned state freed of craving and rebirth. The arahant is one in whom the burning (*pariḷāha*) is gone: 'no burning/fever is found' (Dhp 90). The Ādittapariyāya (Fire Sermon) frames all sensory experience as on fire with passion, aversion, and delusion; nibbāna is the going out of those fires. The Abhidharma schools systematise this as the *asaṃskṛta-dhātu*, the unconditioned element, a real *dharma* distinct from all conditioned (*saṃskṛta*) phenomena. Throughout, the tradition insists on *anātman*: there is no self that is liberated and no absolute into which one merges. The going-out is total and the description remains negative — or is cautiously called 'the unborn, the unconditioned, the unbecome, the unfabricated.'

Nāgārjuna's Madhyamaka radicalises the apophasis. Nirvāṇa is defined by eightfold negation: 'unabandoned, unattained, uncut-off, non-eternal, unceased, unarisen' (MMK 25.3) — refusing both the eternalist reading (nirvāṇa as an existent absolute) and the nihilist reading (nirvāṇa as mere annihilation). Most radically, there is 'no distinction whatever of saṃsāra from nirvāṇa' (MMK 25.19): the distinction between conditioned becoming and unconditioned liberation is itself an expression of conceptual proliferation (*prapañca*), and nirvāṇa is the stilling of that proliferation. Candrakīrti and the Prāsaṅgika tradition press this further: even the Abhidharma's 'unconditioned *dharma*' is a subtle reification. Nothing is positively predicated of nirvāṇa; the image of a flame going out suits this usage because it names no result-state to be grasped.

The bare word *nirvāṇa* does not function as an independent key term in Advaita Vedānta; its role in these traditions is carried by the *Gītā*'s compound *brahma-nirvāṇa* (see that entry). In the *Gītā* (2.72, 5.24–26, 6.15) the going-out image is joined to *brahman*: what goes out is the desire-and-ego fire; what is reached is Brahman. Śaṅkara reads *brahma-nirvāṇa* as the *brahma-bhūta* state — the knower become Brahman — with the *pariḷāha* of conditioning dissolved. Here the word appears as a term being compounded with *brahman*, rather than as an autonomous concept.
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (word compounded with Brahman; see brahmanirvana entry)

textual_history.summary: The verbal root nir + √vā ('to blow out') is old Indo-Aryan: it appears in the Ṛgveda of cooling the cremation fire (RV 10.16.13, *nir vāpayā*), which is the literal verb, not the soteriological noun. As a liberation term, *nirvāṇa* is characteristically Buddhist (and Jain) and is not securely attested in any Hindu text that predates Buddhism; the early principal Upaniṣads do not contain the noun in their root text, and where it appears attached to them (Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Aitareya, Māṇḍūkya in our files) it is in post-Buddhist commentary (Śaṅkara, ~700 CE) or the Gauḍapāda-kārikā (~6th c. CE). In the Pali canon the word *nibbāna* names the arahant's freedom from burning; the Dhammapada arahant-verse (Dhp 90) gives the image of no burning remaining. Nāgārjuna's Madhyamaka defines nirvāṇa through eightfold negation (MMK 25.3) and asserts its non-difference from saṃsāra (MMK 25.19). The earliest Hindu root-text use of the liberation noun is the *Bhagavad-Gītā*'s compound *brahma-nirvāṇa* (BG 2.72; 5.24–26) and *nirvāṇa-paramā* (6.15) — and the Gītā is standardly dated after the Buddha. The image of a flame going out can be set beside the Vedic and Upaniṣadic imagery of a fire kept hidden and kindled in the cave of the heart; that contrast of imagery is a matter of shared fire-and-light vocabulary, and is treated under agni, jyoti, guha, hrdaya, and dipa. The stages below list only attestations that contain the word nirvāṇa / nir-√vā.

stages.sanskrit: yaṁ tvam agne samadahas tam u nir vāpayā punaḥ | kiyāmbv atra rohatu pākadūrvā vyalkaśā
stages.rendering: Whom you, O Agni, have burned — cool him (nir vāpayā) again; may the pākadūrvā with its tendrils grow up there.
stages.note: A funeral hymn: the causative imperative *nir vāpayā* ('blow out / cool again') asks Agni to quench the cremation fire so that green growth may return. This is the literal verb nir + √vā in the Ṛgveda — old Indo-Aryan, pre-Buddhist — and not the soteriological noun. It establishes only that the verbal root is inherited; the liberation noun *nirvāṇa* is not attested in the securely pre-Buddhist Hindu texts (see summary).

stages.sanskrit: Gataddhino visokassa, vippamuttassa sabbadhi; Sabbaganthappahīnassa, pariḷāho na vijjati.
stages.rendering: For one whose journey is done, who is sorrowless, wholly freed, who has abandoned all bonds — no burning/fever (pariḷāho) is found.
stages.note: Pali text. The arahant in whom the fire/burning (pariḷāha) is gone: the going-out sense of *nibbāna* as a lived attainment — the simple going-out of the fever of craving and becoming.

stages.sanskrit: aprahīṇam asaṃprāptam anucchinnam aśāśvatam | aniruddham anutpannam etan nirvāṇam ucyate
stages.rendering: Unabandoned, unattained, uncut-off, non-eternal, unceased, unarisen — this is called nirvāṇa.
stages.note: The eightfold-negation definition. Nāgārjuna declines both the eternalist (nirvāṇa = existent absolute) and the nihilist (nirvāṇa = annihilation) reading. The image of a flame going out suits this usage because it names no result-state.

stages.sanskrit: na saṃsārasya nirvāṇāt kiṃcid asti viśeṣaṇam
stages.rendering: There is no distinction whatever of saṃsāra from nirvāṇa.
stages.note: Madhyamaka non-substantialism: the distinction itself is conceptual proliferation (*prapañca*). Nirvāṇa is not a separate ontological realm one reaches; it is the stilling of grasping at any such distinction.

stages.rendering: Established in it even at the last hour, one comes to the going-out into Brahman (brahma-nirvāṇa).
stages.note: The word *nirvāṇa* compounded with *brahman*: the going-out of the desire-and-ego fire is at once an attaining of Brahman. The earliest Hindu root-text use of the liberation noun, in a text standardly dated after the Buddha. This verse is cited here to mark the cross-reference; the full treatment of *brahma-nirvāṇa* as a compound is in the separate entry brahmanirvana.json.

translator_note: Render *nirvāṇa* / nir-√vā with the going-out image — 'the going-out of the fires', 'blown out', 'gone out' — rather than 'extinction', which in English connotes destruction; the image is a flame quietly going out as its fuel is withdrawn. Do not collapse Buddhist *nirvāṇa* into Vedāntic *mokṣa*: the early Buddhist usage is apophatic and does not affirm an *ātman* or *brahman* as the thing liberated or the substrate attained, so 'liberation' or 'salvation' import assumptions the early tradition does not state. The *Gītā*'s *brahma-nirvāṇa* (separate entry) joins the word to Brahman as the absolute reached; bare *nirvāṇa* (no substrate stated) and *brahma-nirvāṇa* (Brahman reached) should be kept distinct in translation. On priority: the soteriological noun is not securely attested in any Hindu text predating Buddhism, and named scholarship gives the Buddhists terminological priority — Steven Collins writes that 'the Buddhists seem to have been the first to call it nirvāṇa' (Nirvana and Other Buddhist Felicities, 1998). The verbal root nir + √vā is older and shared (RV 10.16.13).
